questions.question: Explain why overriding the `hashCode()` method is crucial when storing user-defined objects in a `HashSet`. What are the potential consequences of not doing so?
keyPoints: The `hashCode()` method is used by hash-based collections like `HashSet` and `HashMap` to determine the bucket location for an object.

If `hashCode()` is not overridden, the default implementation from `Object` is used, which typically converts the memory address of the object into an integer, making two logically equal objects (as defined by `equals()`) have different hash codes.

This leads to duplicate entries in the `HashSet` because the collection will place the two equal objects in different buckets and will not recognize them as duplicates, violating the core contract of a Set.

questions.question: Describe the contract between the `equals(Object obj)` and `hashCode()` methods. What are the key rules that must be followed when overriding these methods for a user-defined class intended for use in collections?
keyPoints: The fundamental rule is that if two objects are equal according to the `equals(Object)` method, then calling the `hashCode()` method on each of the two objects must produce the same integer result.

It is not required that two unequal objects have different hash codes, but it is highly recommended for better performance in hash-based collections to avoid many objects ending up in the same bucket (hash collision).
The `hashCode()` method must consistently return the same integer, provided no information used in `equals` comparisons on the object is modified.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the `Comparable` interface, and how does it differ from the `Comparator` interface when sorting user-defined objects in a collection like a `TreeSet`?
keyPoints: The `Comparable` interface provides a single, natural ordering for objects of a class by implementing the `compareTo(T o)` method. It is defined within the class itself.
The `Comparator` interface is separate from the class and provides a way to define multiple, external ordering strategies by implementing the `compare(T o1, T o2)` method.

A `TreeSet` (or `Collections.sort()`) can use either: it will use the natural order defined by `Comparable` by default, or it can be explicitly given a `Comparator` object at construction to use a custom order instead.
